title: Earable Authentication via Acoustic Toothprint
venue: Proceedings of the 2021 ACM SIGSAC Conference on Computer and Communications Security (CCS ’21)

method.input_representation: MFCC (with delta and delta-delta), Pitch, log spectrum
method.training_strategy: Feature extraction from segmented gesture signals followed by DNN classification

evaluation_protocol: Each participant performed each of the 10 gestures 5 times for enrollment and at least 40 times for authentication testing.

reported_limitations: Canine gestures result in lower accuracy due to less information and difficulty in performance

key_contribution: Proposed ToothSonic, using acoustic toothprints via teeth gestures for earable authentication
Leveraged the occlusion effect of the ear canal and inward-facing microphones to capture attenuated sonic waves
main_strength: High inherent security due to the private teeth-ear channel (skull/tissue modulation)
main_weakness: Lack of standard biometric metrics (EER, FAR, FRR)
No experimental verification of spoofing resistance despite theoretical claims
insights.notes_for_review: Paper reports Accuracy (up to 99.81% for 6 gestures), but target metrics AUC/EER/FRR/FAR are missing.
